compiled_state.title: As a planning pipeline, I route high-blast-radius epics to COMPLEX using cross-stack structural signals
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Description

**What**: Add a blast-radius scoring function to the complexity evaluator that uses cross-stack structural signals to force COMPLEX routing for high-blast-radius epics.
**Why**: The evaluator classified a 51-file, 5-layer cleanup epic as MODERATE because it had zero interface changes and high scope_certainty. The routing missed that pervasive, subtractive changes across many layers are inherently high-blast-radius regardless of interface count.
**Scope**:
- IN: Blast-radius scoring function using path depth, known config/entry/wiring file names (~40 cross-stack patterns), directory conventions, layer count, and change type (additive/subtractive/substitutive). Threshold-based COMPLEX forcing. Validation tests with fixtures from 4+ tech stacks (Go, TypeScript, Python, and one of Rust/Java/Ruby).
- OUT: AST parsing, language-specific tooling, import fan-in counting, project-specific safeguard file lists.

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, the complexity evaluator scores blast radius using structural signals (path depth, known file names, directory conventions, layer count, change type) and forces COMPLEX when the score exceeds a threshold
  ← Satisfies: "The complexity evaluator uses a blast-radius scoring function with cross-stack structural signals to force COMPLEX routing"
- When this story is complete, the scoring function's signals are validated by tests with directory fixtures representing at least 4 different tech stacks
  ← Satisfies: "signals validated across at least 4 tech stacks"
- When this story is complete, unit tests are written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Testing] Fixtures must represent real directory structures from Go (kubernetes-style), TypeScript (next.js-style), Python (pyproject-style), and Rust/Java/Ruby projects
- [Maintainability] The ~40 cross-stack file name patterns need to be maintainable — consider a data-driven approach (list of patterns in a config) rather than hardcoded conditionals

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Escalate to the user whenever you do not have high confidence in your understanding of the work, approach, or intent. "High confidence" means clear evidence from the codebase or ticket context — not inference or reasonable assumption. When in doubt, stop and ask rather than guess.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Adversarial Review Finding
[Implicit Shared State] Story 1 and Story 4 both touch the evaluator-to-implementation-plan integration surface. Story 1's blast-radius output schema must be documented as a contract. Story 4's file impact scan is intentionally independent of blast-radius signals — document this explicitly so future maintainers know the independence is by design, not oversight.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body:
## Adversarial Review Finding
[Conflicting Assumptions] Story 1's blast-radius scoring uses ~40 known file patterns. Story 4's file impact scan uses codebase analysis. These are intentionally different mechanisms at different pipeline stages (routing vs task planning). Document that blast-radius patterns are a routing heuristic, not a comprehensive file list — implementation-plan's independent scan is the authoritative file enumeration.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
